summary: I was living in Baltimore, Maryland when I entered the Service in April 1942. Before joining, I was going to school there and working for Kraft Foods. Baltimore was my hometown and the place where I began my journey toward the medical field, inspired partly by Johns Hopkins University being located there.

Maryland was home to me during the early years of my life and service. I trained at Fort Meade and Walter Reed Hospital in Bethesda, Maryland, where I graduated as a registered male nurse in 1943. My connection to Maryland was strong, as it was the state where I received my medical training and began my military career.

Johns Hopkins University was a familiar name to me growing up in Baltimore. Although I never attended, it represented the medical field I aspired to join. My goal was to work in medicine, inspired by my mother who was a nurse, and Johns Hopkins was a symbol of that ambition.

Before entering the Service, I worked for Kraft Foods in Baltimore. My job there helped me qualify as a medical technician for the Army, which influenced my military occupational specialty. I stayed with Kraft Foods for twenty-five years after the war, so it was a significant part of my life both before and after my military service.

entity: Walter Reed National Military Medical Center
summary: I graduated as a registered male nurse in 1943 at Walter Reed Hospital in Bethesda, Maryland. My medical training there was extensive and prepared me for my role in the Army Air Corps. Walter Reed was where I solidified my skills before being sent overseas.

I enlisted right after the attack on Pearl Harbor in December 1941. That event was unforgettable and motivated me to serve my country. It was a turning point that pushed me to join the military despite my personal challenges.

I initially wanted to join the United States Navy because my cousin was in it and traveled the world. However, they rejected me due to my glasses and an umbilical hernia. The Navy was my first choice, but ultimately, I served in the Army Air Corps instead.
cues: rejection due to glasses and hernia

summary: I served in the United States Army Air Forces during World War II, starting in 1942. I trained in medical fields and was assigned to the 225th Medical Dispensary Aviation unit. My service took me from the U.S. to North Africa, India, and China, where I supported air operations medically.

My basic training began at Fort Meade, Maryland, which was close to my home. It was my first experience away from Baltimore and introduced me to military life. The time there was short before I was sent to further training in Florida and Maryland.

After Fort Meade, I was sent to Tampa, Florida, for Air Corps training. Tampa was where I received additional military instruction before going to Walter Reed for medical training. It was a key stop in my preparation for overseas deployment.

Florida was part of my military training journey, specifically in Tampa and Drew Field. At Drew Field, near Tampa, I took more Air Corps training before being sent overseas. Florida was an important training location during my early service.

I was sent to Drew Field, Florida, after Walter Reed to continue my Air Corps training. Drew Field was adjacent to Tampa and was where I prepared for deployment overseas. It was part of the rapid training process during the war.

I was initially worried I might be assigned to Aberdeen Proving Ground in Maryland because of its work with poison gases and toxic weapons. I did not want to be involved in that kind of work, as I wanted to help rather than harm. Fortunately, I was assigned to medical duties instead.

Our original orders were to participate in the invasion of Sicily during World War II. We left from Hampton Roads, Virginia, on a Liberty ship bound for Sicily, but our ship was sunk by German submarines in the Atlantic. After being rescued and hospitalized in Oran, North Africa, our orders changed and we never made it to Sicily.

We departed from Hampton Roads, Virginia, on a Liberty ship heading to Sicily. It was from this port that our ill-fated journey began, ending with our ship being sunk by German submarines. Hampton Roads was the launching point for that mission.

Virginia was where Hampton Roads is located, the port from which we sailed on a Liberty ship during the war. It was the starting point for our mission to Sicily before the ship was sunk. So, Virginia was part of the early phase of my overseas deployment.

I traveled on a Liberty ship from Hampton Roads, Virginia, bound for the invasion of Sicily. Unfortunately, our ship was attacked and sunk by German submarines in the Atlantic Ocean. We were rescued and taken to a hospital in Oran, North Africa, but never reached Sicily.

Our Liberty ship was sunk by two German submarines in the Atlantic during World War II. It was a terrifying experience, and we were left floating in oil before being rescued. This attack changed our mission and led to hospitalization in Oran, North Africa.

After our ship was sunk by German submarines, we were rescued and taken to a hospital in Oran, North Africa. It was there that I recuperated before receiving new orders to go to India. Oran was a critical recovery point in my wartime journey.

North Africa was where I was hospitalized in Oran after our ship was sunk. It was a brief but important stop before being sent on to India. North Africa was part of the larger theater of operations during the war.

After recovering in North Africa, I was sent to India as part of my overseas service. We were formed as a unit in Bombay and then moved to Calcutta. India was a key base for our medical aviation unit during the war.

Our journey to India took us through the Suez Canal and the Red Sea. This route was part of the long trip from North Africa to the Indian subcontinent. The Suez Canal was a strategic passage during the war.

We traveled through the Red Sea on our way to India after leaving North Africa. The Red Sea was part of the maritime route that connected us to the Indian theater of operations. It was a significant leg of our overseas deployment.

I served most of my time with the 225th Medical Dispensary Aviation unit. We were formed in Bombay, India, and then moved to Calcutta. Our work involved medical support for aviation operations, and we operated in challenging environments including the Burma Road and China.

Our unit was formed in Bombay, India, which is on the west coast. From there, we traveled by troop train across India to Calcutta on the east coast. Bombay was the starting point for our operations in the region.

After forming in Bombay, we moved to Calcutta by troop train. Calcutta was our base on the east coast of India and a hub for our medical aviation unit. From there, we supported operations along the Burma Road and into China.

We traveled the Burma Road from Ledo in Burma to Kunming in China. The road was treacherous, with narrow precipices and dangerous driving conditions that claimed many lives. It was a vital supply route but very hazardous for our ambulances and vehicles.

Ledo was the starting point in Burma for the Burma Road journey to China. We drove ambulances and supplies from Ledo over the dangerous mountain roads. Ledo was a critical junction in the supply chain to China during the war.

Burma was part of the route we took on the Burma Road from Ledo to China. The terrain was extremely dangerous with narrow roads and high precipices. Burma was a challenging but essential area for our medical and supply operations.

Kunming was the endpoint of the Burma Road in China. We traveled there by truck from Burma and then flew to other locations like Chengdu. Kunming was a key city in the China theater where we operated during the war.
I spent time in Chengdu, which is north of Kunming in central China. We built our hospital there and operated from that area. Chengdu was an important base for our medical unit in the China theater.

The Himalayas were a formidable natural barrier we had to fly over to supply China. Because heavy equipment couldn't be flown over the mountains, they had to be disassembled and transported in parts. The Himalayas made logistics extremely difficult in the China-Burma-India theater.

We faced the threat of Japanese zero fighters in the China theater. The Japanese zero was a formidable enemy aircraft during the war. Our medical unit operated under the constant risk of enemy air attacks.

The Lockheed P-38 Lightning was one of the aircraft types operating in the China-Burma-India theater. Our medical unit supported air operations involving planes like the P-38. It was part of the air power we worked alongside during the war.
Dick Silviera was not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal experience or connection with him during my service.

President Franklin D. Roosevelt was leading the United States during the war. His administration was responsible for programs like Reverse Lend-Lease, which supported troops like us in China with supplies and funding. Roosevelt's policies impacted our living conditions and operations overseas.

Reverse Lend-Lease was a program during World War II where the U.S. provided money and supplies to support allied forces, including us in China. It helped pay for food, billeting, and other necessities. We lived off what was locally available, like cabbage or peanuts, depending on the indigenous crops, thanks to this program.
